LLM, RAG & Memory
Why the model decides, not the code
Fundamentals · 18 min read
TerranSoul in six scenes — how LLMs, RAG, memory and reasoning become a useful daily assistant.

If you are getting lost between a model, a chatbot, RAG, memory and TerranSoul, you are not alone. They are not six names for the same thing. Here is a non-technical walkthrough — six everyday scenes — that shows what each layer is for, and why TerranSoul is more than choosing free, paid or local AI.

Far more than a chat box. ChatGPT is OpenAI's flagship product family — web/desktop/mobile chat, voice mode, Canvas for writing & code, Codex for software engineering, custom GPTs, Operator for agentic browsing, and the Memory feature for long-running context.

Anthropic's full ecosystem: Claude chat (web/desktop/mobile), Claude Code (terminal & IDE coding agent), Claude Cowork for shared spaces, design and Artifacts in-chat, Projects for grouped context, and the MCP-based connector layer.
The progression below — base LLM → RAG look-up → personal memory → connected memory → TerranSoul in daily use.

How TerranSoul does it
If the companion takes 6 seconds to look up "what did we deprecate?", you stop using it.
Sharded vector index, RRF fusion, query-class HyDE, hot caches. Sub-second on millions of memories.
The IDE agent and the 3D companion must agree on what is current. Otherwise you fix the same bug twice.
Append-only versioning + LLM-as-judge conflict resolution + per-memory CAP profile (consensus on legal/financial, eventual on scratch).
If the brain only lives in someone else's cloud, it isn't really your memory — it is theirs, on loan.
Local-first by default. CRDT sync to your own devices. Optional encrypted relay for partners or teammates. Per-memory privacy ACL.
"It feels like it remembers" is not enough. You need to know when it doesn't.
Public benchmark harness (LongMemEval-S, LoCoMo MTEB, agent-memory token-efficiency), telemetry on retrieval health, uptime SLO.

Scene 06 · Harness, Context & Reasoning
Why the model decides, not the code.

Scene 06
Three layers: harness provides tools, context assembles what the model sees, reasoning is the model's own work.
The assistant that builds scaffolding, not behaviour
In Scenes 1–5 we covered what the brain does. This scene explains the engineering discipline behind how — and specifically, why TerranSoul never pre-decides what the model should do.

Most chatbot products contain hidden code that says things like "if the user sends a URL, fetch the page title and inject it before the model sees the message" or "if the input looks like a cooking question, add recipe context automatically." It sounds helpful. In practice, it turns the model into a puppet: every new edge case becomes a code bug rather than something the model can reason about.
TerranSoul takes a different approach, borrowed from frontier AI labs and formalised as three engineering layers:

Harness — the scaffolding layer. Tools the model can call (fetch a URL, search memories, look up the knowledge graph), gates that limit cost or safety, retries when a tool fails, sandboxes for code execution. The harness is pure mechanism: it never decides whether the model should use a tool, only that it can.

Context — the assembly layer. Before every turn, TerranSoul assembles what fits in the prompt window: a RAG slice of relevant documents, a persona slice of long-lived traits, recent conversation turns, and knowledge-graph edges that connect related memories. Context engineering is about selection and budget — which pieces go in, how many tokens each gets — never about pre-answering the question.

Reasoning — the model's own work. Chain of thought, tool calls, and the final answer. This layer belongs entirely to the LLM. TerranSoul's code never simulates reasoning in Rust or TypeScript — it only elicits it by giving the model good tools and good context.

If your code decides what the model should do based on what the user said, you have moved reasoning out of the model and into a regex. Every new edge case is now a code bug instead of a prompt adjustment.
The Knowledge Graph as context, not as answer

The knowledge graph from Scene 4 (memory_edges in SQLite — typed relationships between memory rows) is a perfect example of context engineering done right. At query time, the retriever finds vector-similar memories, then walks one or two hops along graph edges to pull in related facts. These connected facts are assembled into the prompt as context. The model then decides — by reasoning — which facts actually answer the question. The graph never short-circuits the model's reasoning; it only enriches what the model can see.

This means a knowledge graph with 50,000 edges works the same way as one with 500: the code provides structure, the model provides judgment. No keyword routing. No hardcoded "if the user asks about trips, pull all trip nodes." The model sees the neighbourhood, and the model decides what matters.
Why this matters in daily use

You will never see these three layers as a user. You will just notice that the assistant handles unusual questions well, that it does not break when you ask something unexpected, and that it improves when a new tool is added — without needing a code release to "support" that new question type.

For developers building on TerranSoul's MCP surface, the discipline is also practical: you can add a new tool (a new harness capability), and the model will start using it when appropriate, without anyone writing routing logic. Context assembles itself from the memory store. Reasoning happens in the model. The three layers stay cleanly separated.
Maps to

In TerranSoul, this is the harness + context engineering layer. The Rust streaming pipeline (StreamTagParser) provides tools and gates. The RAG pipeline assembles context from vector search, keyword search (FTS5), knowledge-graph hops, and persona traits — all fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion. The model's reasoning happens inside its own chain-of-thought and tool-call blocks. No line of Rust or TypeScript ever substitutes for a model decision.
Under the hood

The architecture enforces this separation through a formal rule: any code that runs between the user message and the LLM call must be infrastructure that runs on every turn regardless of content (rate limiting, token-budget assembly, RAG retrieval, summarisation). Code that is conditional on what the user said — URL detection, topic sniffing, "helpful" pre-fetch — is either exposed as a tool the model can call, or routed through the brain classifier with an ai-decision-policy toggle the user controls. This rule is tested in CI and enforced in code review.

The knowledge graph specifically lives in memory_edges (columns: src_id, dst_id, rel_type, confidence). Multi-hop retrieval is exposed as the multi_hop_search_memories command and the MCP tool brain_kg_neighbors. Edges are extracted automatically from conversations by the LLM itself — the model decides what is a relationship, not a regex. Conflict resolution between contradictory edges uses an LLM-as-judge step, keeping reasoning in the model layer where it belongs.

What it's great at
How TerranSoul composes with it
A broad AI platform — chat, voice, Canvas, Codex for engineering, custom GPTs, Operator for agentic tasks, and built-in Memory.
Independent and powerful on its own. TerranSoul can call OpenAI models as a brain backend and can share notes/decisions with ChatGPT workflows through MCP-style connectors.
A full ecosystem: Claude chat, Claude Code (terminal/IDE coding), Cowork shared spaces, Artifacts, Projects, and a strong MCP story.
Independent and powerful on its own. TerranSoul speaks MCP, so Claude Code can mount the TerranSoul brain as a tool when you want shared project memory across machines.
An open coding-agent UX with messaging bridges — reads files, edits files, runs commands, and can be reached from chat apps and webhooks.
Composes naturally. OpenClaw can plug into TerranSoul's MCP brain for project memory, semantic search and code intelligence.
A self-improving CLI agent with sessions, skills, scheduling, 14 toolsets, 16 messaging gateways, and a desktop GUI.
Composes naturally — TerranSoul is mounted as a first-class MCP server in Hermes's config when you want shared memory.
One specific job: a brain, a persona, a 3D companion, plus the privacy and sync layer that lets the brain travel with you.
Lends itself to all of the above, so a decision you made yesterday in Claude Code is visible to OpenClaw or Hermes tomorrow.
